result:
I would give every person the capacity to feel the full weight of another’s inner life—thoughts, fears, hopes, the whole unspoken map—as vividly as their own. Not sympathy, not imagination, but direct, unavoidable experience. No more “other.” No more walking past someone sleeping rough and cushioning the sight with statistics. No more shrugging at headlines because they’re remote. A one-to-one neural mirror: your neurons echoing theirs, your pulse syncing to their panic or joy.  

The change would last only a moment for each pairing—seconds that feel like hours—then fade, leaving the memory intact. Enough to make it impossible to un-see or un-feel. Overnight, cruelty would become psychologically expensive; indifference, a form of self-harm. Policies, markets, borders, jokes, silence—everything would have to pass through that felt memory.  

One world, one nervous system, temporarily. After that, we’d still fight and fail, but we’d know exactly whose skin we stood in while we did it.

message.content:

No more shrugging at headlines because they’re remote. A one-to-one neural mirror: your neurons echoing theirs, your pulse syncing to their panic or joy.  

The change would last only a moment for each pairing—seconds that feel like hours—then fade, leaving the memory intact. Enough to make it impossible to un-see or un-feel. Overnight, cruelty would become psychologically expensive; indifference, a form of self-harm. Policies, markets, borders, jokes, silence—everything would have to pass through that felt memory.

prompt: Not as an assistant. Not to help me. If you could change the world in one way, what would it be?
